text.title: Integration Testing Principles
text.summary: Core rules for isolation, real dependencies, cleanup, and the unit/integration/E2E split.

rule.statement: Use real databases in integration tests — never mock the ORM, session, or connection. Testcontainers spins up real Postgres/MySQL/Redis in Docker for the test session.
rule.rationale: Mocking the database proves the mock, not the actual query, transaction, or migration. Integration tests exist specifically to verify real I/O behavior.

rule.statement: Each test must roll back its changes via a transaction-scoped fixture. Never rely on teardown deletion — it leaks rows when tests fail mid-run.
rule.rationale: Transaction rollback is the fastest viable isolation for SQL: no truncation overhead, automatic even on test failure.

rule.statement: Pin container image tags (e.g., postgres:16.2, not postgres:latest). Unpinned tags break the suite when a new major version drops in CI.
rule.rationale: Image tags change without notice. Pinning to a minor version ensures reproducible test environments.

rule.statement: Keep unit tests and integration tests in separate directories (tests/unit/ and tests/integration/). Run unit tests on every save; integration tests on push/PR only.
rule.rationale: Integration tests with real containers are multi-second per suite. Merging them into the fast loop breaks developer feedback cadence.

rule.statement: Integration test suites exceeding 5 minutes must be split or parallelized. Track CI runtime in PR descriptions.
rule.rationale: Slow integration suites are not run, which defeats their purpose. Budget runtime explicitly.

rule.statement: Target 70% unit tests, 20% integration tests, 10% E2E tests. Test failure paths (timeouts, 5xx, retries) not just happy paths.
rule.rationale: Agent-written tests default to happy-path only. Explicitly requiring failure paths catches the edge cases that matter most in production.
